metadata.title: Stress-Test the Assignment Before Students Do

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Shalom, Liam — in for Bear. This is Liam, in for Bear? Can you explain it, Bear?

props.command: This is Liam, in for Bear?
props.segment: Stress-Test the Assignment Before Students Do

beats.narration_text: The assignment had been running for four years. Students were submitting work that satisfied every rubric item and demonstrated almost nothing. The faculty member knew AI was involved — she could not find it. She pasted the assignment into Claude and asked: read this as a student who wants to avoid intellectual work. Where would you shortcut? Claude identified three. She had never seen them. The assignment had always invited them. This is Liam, in for Bear.
beats.new_visual_element: ClaudeComposerAsk — cold open, stress-test assignment prompt

props.command: Read this assignment as a student who wants to avoid intellectual work. Where are the shortcuts?

beats.narration_text: The stress-test prompt has three parts. Read this as a student who would rather use AI than do the intellectual work. Identify every place in the prompt where AI output would satisfy the requirement without demonstrating student learning. Suggest three redesign moves that build in process evidence or make the core intellectual work harder to outsource. Claude reads the assignment as an adversarial user. That adversarial read is the audit.
beats.new_visual_element: Three-part audit — adversarial read / identify shortcuts / redesign moves

beats.narration_text: The common findings: prompts that ask for summary rather than analysis, rubric items that can be satisfied by fluent prose without the underlying argument, word counts that AI can hit without the thinking that word counts were supposed to proxy. Claude finds these because it knows how to satisfy them. It has read thousands of examples of work that satisfies rubrics without demonstrating learning. It is the most honest reader you can give an assignment to.
beats.new_visual_element: Common shortcut findings — summary vs analysis / rubric satisfiable without argument / word-count proxy

beats.narration_text: The redesign moves Claude suggests are not about restricting AI use — they are about building in process evidence. A reflection paragraph documenting a decision made during the work. A draft plus revision with visible changes. A presentation that requires live explanation of choices. An artifact that names the specific source the student read, not the topic. These moves make the intellectual work observable — and they are much harder to outsource than a finished product.
beats.new_visual_element: Process evidence moves — reflection / draft+revision / live explanation / named source

beats.narration_text: This is one of the highest-leverage uses of Claude for faculty: asking it to stress-test the assignment before students do. An AI-use policy tells students what they cannot do. A stress-tested assignment gives students nothing to outsource without losing the grade. The audit takes fifteen minutes. The redesign insight it produces is the kind that previously required watching a hundred student submissions fail in the same pattern.
beats.new_visual_element: Artifact view — policy vs. stress-test: policy restricts / audit removes the shortcut

beats.narration_text: Stress-test one assignment this week. Paste this.

props.command: Read this assignment as a student who would rather complete it with minimal intellectual effort, possibly using AI tools. Identify: (1) every place where AI output satisfies the requirement without demonstrating learning, (2) the two easiest shortcuts a student could use, (3) three redesign moves that build in process evidence or make the core intellectual work harder to outsource. [paste assignment here]

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Stress-Test the Assignment Before Students Do] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
